data.title: These Stocks Are Moving the Most Today: Warner Bros., Paramount Skydance, Tesla, Super Micro, Microsoft, Figure Technology, and More — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Joe Woelfel and Elsa Ohlen

Stocks were mostly higher Friday after the Dow Jones Industrial Average crossed 46,000 for the first time Thursday and set a record high, joining the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite at all-time closing highs following U.S. inflation data that likely won't prevent the Federal Reserve from cutting interest rates when it meets next week.

These stocks were making moves Friday:

Warner Bros. Discovery rose 14% following the stock's surge Thursday of 29% — its largest daily percentage increase on record — after The Wall Street Journal reported Paramount Skydance was preparing a takeover bid that would be backed by the Ellison family. Paramount's majority cash bid would be for the entire company including its cable networks and movie studio, the Journal reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

Paramount Skydance, meanwhile, was up 5.4% to $18.41. Earlier in the session the stock set a 52-week high of $18.56.

Tesla rose 5.7%. The move followed a gain of 6% on Thursday for shares of the electric-vehicle maker, which rose in part on U.S. inflation data that was in line with economists' estimates and that firmed up the belief the Federal Reserve will be cutting interest rates next week. Since cars are generally financed, auto companies benefit from lower rates.

Super Micro Computer rose 2.8% after beginning shipments of its artificial-intelligence Blackwell Ultra solutions powered by Nvidia chips to customers worldwide. The systems includes Nvidia's plug-and-play HGX B300 system and GB300 NVL72, which are used for training AI models. Nvidia was up 0.2%.

Adobe was up 0.2% after posting fiscal third-quarter earnings and revenue that beat Wall Street estimates and issuing forecasts for the fourth quarter and fiscal year that also topped expectations. The creative-software company said 99% of Fortune 100 companies have used artificial intelligence in an Adobe app, and more than 40% of its top 50 enterprise accounts doubled their annualized recurring revenue spending since the start of fiscal 2023. Coming into Friday, Adobe has declined 21% this year.

Figma, an Adobe rival, was down 2.5% following a 9.6% jump Thursday. A deal for Adobe to acquire Figma for $20 billion in 2022 fell through on regulatory concerns, particularly from he European Commission and the U.K. Competition and Markets Authority.

Microsoft rose 1.6% after reaching a deal to extend its partnership with OpenAI. The current AI investment boom began with ChatGPT's debut in late 2022, and it wouldn't have been possible without funding from Microsoft and access to its Azure cloud. But the relationship has frayed, as both companies' AI ambitions outgrew their partnership.

RH was down 2.4% after the home furnishings retailer's second-quarter earnings widely missed consensus estimates and revenue came up short of Wall Street targets. The company also reduced its current fiscal-year revenue outlook, saying it expects revenue growth of 9% to 11%, down from its prior guidance of 10% to 13%. RH said its updated outlook "reflects a $30 million cost of incremental tariffs, net of mitigation, in the second half."

Figure Technology Solutions was rising 11% to $34.67. Shares of the company, which uses blockchain technology to help generate home equity lines of credit, opened Thursday at $36 and ended the session at $31.11, a gain of 24% over its initial public offering price of $25.

Shares of Opendoor Technologies fell 9% after the real estate tech platform closed Thursday at $10.48, up 79% during the session, marking the stock's largest daily percentage increase on record. Driving the stock higher was the naming of Shopify Chief Operating Officer Kaz Nejatian as its new chief executive and reappointing co-founders Keith Rabois and Eric Wu to the board. Coming into Friday trading, Opendoor shares have risen nearly 560% this year.

Write to Joe Woelfel at joseph.woelfel@barrons.com

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1121 ET - A potential merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery would likely clear regulatory scrutiny, according to Benchmark. The timing is "particularly auspicious" says analyst Daniel Kurnos. He says the FCC's September preliminary agenda seeks to potentially eliminate the rule prohibiting dual ownership of broadcast networks. While CBS is the only broadcast network and station group involved in this potential deal, it "suggests an openness to further media consolidation of the major players," Kurnos says. The Ellison family's close family ties with the Trump Administration will likely also help avoid potential obstacles to the merger, Benchmark says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1040 ET - A Paramount Skydance/Warner Bros. Discovery merger "could yield meaningful upside for the combined entity," says Benchmark analyst Daniel Kurnos. Two years ago, Benchmark estimated a merger between the two companies would create synergies between $3.15 billion and $4.9 billion, although the companies have since begun their own cost reduction programs. "The math remains relatively the same, and the industrial logic has only gotten stronger in what appears to be a consolidating media landscape," Kurnos says. The analyst adds that even without synergies, "Paramount could pay $18 per share for Warner and still have the deal be value neutral or possibly even marginally accretive." Warner Bros. Discovery surges 12%, while Paramount Skydance is up 4%. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0920 ET - A Paramount Skydance merger with Warner Bros. Discovery could get more difficult if Paramount doesn't act quickly, says Benchmark analyst Matthew Harrigan. The Warner Bros. Discovery separation is planned for 2Q26, which would make a Paramount bid for the company tax challenged for 12 months, "providing an impetus for a serious near-term offer," Harrigan says. The separate Warner Bros. and Discovery Global stocks could also get more expensive as Harrigan says investors are currently undervaluing the company's earnings potential with the Studio and HBO Max divisions set for renewed growth in the wake of an eventual $5 billion in cost reductions. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0907 ET - Sony, Netflix, Apple and Amazon could all be potential bidders for parts of Warner Bros. Discovery, but another suitor is unlikely to challenge Paramount Skydance's acquisition bid, says Benchmark analyst Matthew Harrigan. Paramount Skydance has privileged access to capital and could create more synergies with a merger than other potential bidders. The company is also best positioned to manage the decline in linear television networks while transitioning to streaming distribution. Sony is unlikely to challenge an Ellison family bid, Netflix is wary of old media influences and Apple and Amazon could be turned off by share price impact, Harrigan says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0825 ET - Mizuho analysts say in a research note that they expect Applied Materials to lose market share. The semiconductor-equipment maker's revenue is largely exposed to segments that are facing increased competition from China, the analysts say. As a result, Applied Materials is losing customers. This comes after the company last month forecast a drop in profit and revenue this quarter, hurt by challenges in China and choppy demand across end markets. Mizuho downgrades Applied Materials to neutral and lowers its price target to $175 from $200. Applied Materials is off 2% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0759 ET - Adobe logs better-than-expected F3Q results, with its small-but-quickly growing AI-infused product portfolio hitting its annual recurring revenue goal a quarter early, KeyBanc says. "The outlook for next quarter is moving higher, but we are not yet certain the green-shoots are there for a full-fledged, material acceleration in FY26 at this point," analysts Jackson Ader and Jack Nichols say in a research note. While there are many AI model builders developing their own potentially competitive tools according to the analysts, they say Adobe nevertheless remains the center of gravity for the creative process. Adobe shares gain 3% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0659 ET - Adobe is fighting an uphill battle, attempting to prove to investors that AI isn't a threat to its core value proposition. The software company did so in its latest quarter, Davidson analysts say in a research note, as it outperformed Wall Street expectations and effectively drove adoption of its expanding AI-infused product portfolio. This momentum around usage and adoption supports a boost to its outlook, they add. "The company also continues to actively buy back shares at current levels and deliver mid-40% operating margins, supporting double digits earnings growth," the analysts write. Davidson maintains its buy rating, and Adobe shares climb 3.8% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0656 ET - Adobe's beat-and-raise in its fiscal 3Q doesn't ease long-term concerns, Melius Research analysts say in a note. "The story at the end of the day is revenue growth could decelerate next year," they write, adding that the software company's 4Q guide calls for an on-year slowdown in sales despite price hikes. Adobe benefited from higher prices in its latest quarter, but pricing doesn't seem sustainable given increasing competition. Management notes that competition from Figma, Canva and bigger technology companies such as Meta Platforms doesn't seem to be affecting the lower-end of the market yet. Melius maintains its sell rating on the stock. Adobe shares rise 3.6%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0011 ET - Service Stream's bulls at Macquarie are enthused by the infrastructure-services provider's A$1.6 billion defense contract, telling clients that it opens up a whole new vertical for the Australian company. Macquarie analysts write in a note that they hadn't expected Service Stream to win in two regions, or for the award to be this large. The contract represents a 10% increase to their prior fiscal 2027 revenue forecast and puts Service Stream in a good position to secure additional work that drive incremental revenue growth, they add. Macquarie lifts its target price 12% to A$2.70 and keeps an outperform rating on the stock, which is down 0.7% at A$2.305. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2127 ET - President Trump's plan to impose "fairly substantial" tariffs on semiconductor imports poses risks for Malaysia, a top global exporter, TA Securities analyst Chan Mun Chun says in a note. Malaysia exported MYR60.6 billion worth of semiconductors to the U.S. in 2024, and higher costs could weigh on exports and demand. Still, the impact may be cushioned as about 65% of shipments come from U.S. firms in Malaysia that could qualify for exemptions, he notes. Given the complex supply chain, more exemptions are likely, and repatriating production would take decades. Local players under TA Securities' coverage Inari Amertron, Unisem (M) and Malaysian Pacific Industries have limited direct U.S. exposure but may face indirect pressure if global end-demand softens, he adds. TA Securities has a neutral rating on Malaysia's semiconductor sector. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1943 ET - Service Stream's premium valuation relative to its Australia-listed peers looks justified to its bulls at Citi. The investment bank's analysts tell clients in a note that there are multiple near-to-medium term upside catalysts that could help the infrastructure-services provider maintain solid earnings growth. They are pleasantly surprised by the scale of Service Stream's new defense contracts and also think that its utilities margin could continue to surprise to the upside. Service Stream also has the balance sheet to pursue M&A, they add. Citi raises its target price 8.2% to A$2.65 and keeps a buy rating on the stock, which is at A$2.32 ahead of the open. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1814 ET [Dow Jones]--Service Stream gets a new bull in Ord Minnett after it was awarded a Base Services Contract with Australia's defense department. The contract covers South Australia and Northern Territory. It is valued at A$1.6 billion over six years initially, with the potential to be extended by up to four years. "Once the contract is fully deployed, and overlaying margin and capex assumptions, our FY 2027 Ebitda forecasts are upgraded by 8% and EPS forecasts upgraded by 11%," says analyst Ian Munro. The contract win supports Ord Minnett's expectation that Service Stream's return on equity can expand to 14.6% in FY 2027, from 13.4% in FY 2025. It upgrades the stock to buy, from accumulate. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1006 ET - Roughly half--48%--of newly built apartments completed in 1Q were rented within three months, according to Redfin. That is up from 47% in 4Q 2024 and 46% the quarter before that. While the recent uptick in the rental absorption rate is small, it's notable because for much of 2021-2023, the absorption rate was declining. It's likely inching up now because the number of new apartments hitting the market is falling. Renters have fewer options to choose from, making it easier for landlords to fill units fast. As many Americans find themselves priced out of homeownership, rents are beginning to rise again. Apartment building permits have fallen below pre-pandemic levels. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0929 ET - The current offer put forward by BBVA is sufficiently attractive for Sabadell shareholders to accept it, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note. BBVA, which last year launched a hostile bid for its smaller rival, opened the acceptance period to Sabadell shareholders earlier this week. Sabadell's board has recommended that shareholders don't tender their shares. "Both banks are arriving at very different answers and, in reality, the answer is probably somewhere in the middle," analyst Benjamin Toms writes. There is potential to create value out of a deal even if it isn't as large or quickly realizable as BBVA estimates, he notes. To avoid being left with an acceptance rate between 30% and 50%, BBVA could still boost the price of the offer by around 10%, he calculates. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0829 ET - Italian banks are in a good place with loan and fee growth picking up and consolidation scenarios still to play out, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods say in a research note. "The Italian banks continue to live some of their best lives, with strong [returns on tangible equity] and high payout ratios, but also dynamic management teams that continue to work hard to improve their businesses," analysts Hugo Cruz and Ben Maher write after a trip to Milan. They point to more evidence of a pick up in corporate loan growth and stable or easing loan pricing competition, supporting net interest income trends. The market might need more clarity on the outcome of Monte dei Paschi's Mediobanca takeover before another potential M&A round, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0624 ET - The current price that BBVA is offering for Sabadell is insufficient, says a Sabadell board member that represents its third largest shareholder. Fintech Advisory holds a 3.86% stake in the Spanish bank. "In my opinion, the transaction presented by BBVA is the right strategy for both institutions, although at a price that currently makes it unfeasible," David Martinez Guzman says. He adds that he agrees with the board's rejection of the offer and won't tender the shares he owns or those he manages through the fund. BBVA should reconsider and submit a competitive offer that will allow it to get at least 50% of Sabadell shareholders on board, he says. Sabadell issued its formal opinion on the bid and is recommending that shareholders don't tender their shares to the $18 billion takeover offer. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0515 ET - The Indonesian government's plan to inject IDR200 trillion into state-owned banks may ease investor concerns over liquidity conditions and rising fund costs, says Nomura analyst Tushar Mohata. The cash injection would boost sector-wide excess liquidity, meaning deposits minus loans, by 37%. That would be good for major lenders, whose shares have been under persistent selling pressure, with valuations well below historical averages, Mohata adds. Nomura thinks SOE banks, particularly Bank Rakyat Indonesia, Bank Mandiri and Bank Negara Indonesia are well-positioned to deploy new liquidity into higher-margin segments, while Bank Syariah Indonesia needs more funding discipline. It retains buy calls on all four. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0355 ET - Indonesia's plan to inject 200 trillion rupiah into state-owned banks isn't likely to meaningfully lift loan growth, DBS Group Research says. Overall loan demand is likely weakening, with investment loan demand showing no signs of accelerating and working capital demand still soft. Banks are also expected to stay cautious on consumer loans amid asset-quality concerns. "Given the lack of structural demand-side support, we do not expect this policy to significantly change the credit growth trajectory," they say. DBS reiterates a neutral rating on Indonesia's banking sector and prefers Bank Jago and Bank Central Asia. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

2148 ET - Singapore banks' net interest margin are expected to be pressured this year and 1H of 2026 amid low interest rates and liquidity inflows, UOB Kay Hian analyst Jonathan Koh writes in a report. "Singapore has become a magnet for liquidity seeking shelter," and this is expected to impact the banks, he says. The Singapore Overnight Rate Average--a benchmark for pricing floating-rate mortgage rates--recently dropped to a low of 0.73% amid Indonesia's social unrest and series of demonstrations in late August. The stubbornly low domestic interest rates, which look to be structural in nature, are likely to keep NIMs compressed, Koh adds. UOB KH downgrades Singapore's banking sector to market weight.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

2121 ET - DBS Group's "very decent" 2026 dividend yield of 5.1% could rise further on potential capital-return dividends, RHB Research writes in an email. RHB Research estimates the Singapore lender will pay out a 2026 dividend of S$2.70, based on DBS's guidance that its S$0.24 increase in annual dividends should be sustainable next year. Investors are likely to welcome such strong dividend visibility, RHB Research adds. The analyst expects DBS to continue committing to capital returns and dividends, but projects the bank's 2025 profit after tax and minority interests will fall 5% on year. DBS's shares surpassed S$53 on Thursday to notch a new intraday record. The stock trades 0.1% lower at S$52.52. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

2053 ET - Indonesia's planned 200 trillion rupiah fund injection is expected to benefit state-owned lenders, Citi analyst Ferry Wong says in a note. Six banks are expected to receive the liquidity, including Bank Rakyat Indonesia, Bank Mandiri, Bank Negara Indonesia and Bank Tabungan Negara, he notes. The funds, drawn from the government's surplus at Bank Indonesia, equal about 4.5% of total deposits at these state-owned enterprise banks, he reckons. With potential funding costs below market deposit rates, lenders should see improved lending margins, boosting profitability, he adds. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1927 ET [Dow Jones]--Any potential upside to Macquarie's earnings from rebounding capital markets already looks priced in, according to Morgan Stanley analysts. They tell clients in a note that recovering M&A activity presents upside risk to their forecasts, but that the stock is trading at 21.5 times their fiscal 2026 net profit forecast, which compares with a 10-year average of 16. This suggests to them that a capital-markets recovery and Macquarie's structural growth options are already incorporated into investors' outlook. Return-on-equity prospects are also weaker than at Commonwealth Bank, they add. MS keeps an equal-weight rating and A$216.00 target price on the stock, which is at A$219.91 ahead of the open. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1033 ET - Oil futures are recovering from the previous day's losses as the U.K. announces new sanctions on Russia and the U.S. urges Europe to put tariff pressure on buyers of Russian oil, namely India and China. "The Russia/Ukraine war remains a positive to crude prices with Ukraine hitting a major oil export facility in Russia late yesterday," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. "Russia exports nearly 7 million barrels a day, therefore any disruptions whether from tariffs or war conflicts could be a bullish catalyst for crude prices near-term." WTI is up 1.5% at $63.28 a barrel and Brent is up 1.6% at $67.43. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0908 ET - Equinor faces challenges from an expected decline in European gas prices and lower financial flexibility due to its participation in Orsted's rights issue, UBS analysts write. European gas storage has refilled steadily, while at the same time new LNG projects have started up. Longer term, LNG investments are picking up and UBS expects gas prices to drop even further than previously. Meanwhile, the Danish wind-farm developer has confirmed it will participate in the Orsted rights issue to maintain its 10% stake, at a cost of $939 million. "This will further increase gearing and reduce financial flexibility, increasing risks around shareholder returns into next year." UBS cuts its price target on the stock to 210 kroner ($21) from 220 kroner and reiterates its sell rating. Shares rise 0.5% to 242.50 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0342 ET - European oil stocks slip in opening trade as worries about oversupply continue to mount. Brent crude trades down 0.7% at $65.93 a barrel and prices remain under pressure. A surprise inventory build of 3.9 million barrels in the U.S. last week combined with growing concerns around oversupply as OPEC+ members plan to boost production is contributing to traders' worries, Derren Nathan, head of equity research at Hargreaves Lansdown, writes. In London, BP shares fall 0.7% and Shell drops 0.15%. France's TotalEnergies is down 0.65% while shares in Spain's Repsol and Italy's Eni are down around 0.4%. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0011 ET - Service Stream's bulls at Macquarie are enthused by the infrastructure-services provider's A$1.6 billion defense contract, telling clients that it opens up a whole new vertical for the Australian company. Macquarie analysts write in a note that they hadn't expected Service Stream to win in two regions, or for the award to be this large. The contract represents a 10% increase to their prior fiscal 2027 revenue forecast and puts Service Stream in a good position to secure additional work that drive incremental revenue growth, they add. Macquarie lifts its target price 12% to A$2.70 and keeps an outperform rating on the stock, which is down 0.7% at A$2.305. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1518 ET - Crude futures pull back from gains of the previous three sessions with the market continuing to weigh geopolitical risks against bearish views of market balances. The Paris-based IEA raised its supply growth estimates by 200,000 barrels a day for this year and 2026 to 2.7 million b/d and 2.1 million b/d, respectively, while nudging up its smaller demand growth estimates to a lesser extent. OPEC, which is in the process of adding barrels to the market, left its demand growth estimates at 1.29 million b/d for this year and 1.38 million b/d for 2026.WTI settles down 2% at $62.37 a barrel and Brent falls 1.7% to $66.37. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0443 ET - Mining stocks have helped propel the FTSE 100 index to a record high in morning trade in London. Anglo American shares are up nearly 13% over the past 5 days on the news that it has agreed to merge with Teck Resources to create a $53 billion copper giant. Glencore is up 7%. Much of the share-price growth has been concentrated on gold and silver stocks as precious metals continue to benefit from safe-have demand. Fresnillo trades up 7%, Hochschild Mining is up 6%, with Endeavour Mining 4% higher. The FTSE 100 trades up 0.3% at 9,326 points. Over the year-to-date, it is up 14%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0327 ET - Miners lead the gains on the FTSE 100 in opening trade. Silver and gold miner Fresnillo tops the index, with shares trading around 4% higher. Others such as Anglo American, Endeavour Mining and Glencore rise around 2%. The rise comes as gold trades up 0.5% at $3,653 a troy ounce, while silver rises 1.3% to $42.1 an ounce. Gold is being boosted by bets from investors that the U.S. Federal Reserve starts cutting rates, a weaker dollar and continuing geopolitical tensions, Swissquote Bank analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya writes. Thursday's economic data from the U.S. has given the Federal Reserve the green light to cut rates next week, Ozkardeskaya adds. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0242 ET - UBS reckons gold will reach $3,800/oz by the end of 2025, up from a previous forecast of $3,500/oz. By mid-2026, the price could be around $3,900/oz, it reckons. The bank had previously projected $3,700/oz gold mid next year. UBS says expectations that the Federal Reserve will restart its easing cycle due to weak jobs data are fueling investor appetite. Ongoing geopolitical uncertainties are also underpinning demand, it says. UBS expects ETF holdings will rise near a previous record high of just above 3,900 metric tons by the end of 2025. UBS stays long gold in its global asset allocation, it says. Spot gold is up 0.5% at $3,651.49. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0101 ET - Aggregate global commodity prices are tracking--and will likely continue to track--broadly sideways, says HSBC economist Paul Bloxham. "However, this overall picture hides considerable divergence, volatility and market fragmentation below the surface," says Bloxham. He highlights record-high gold prices, volatile copper markets and softer oil prices, among other moves. "'Geoeconomics,' a nexus of geopolitics and economics, is increasingly driving commodity market dynamics" from gold and oil to soy beans and rare earths, says Bloxham. "More price volatility, shorter commodity price cycles and greater market fragmentation are the results," he says. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2255 ET - Perenti's latest contract for underground mining services at Ramelius' Dalgaranga gold project pleases Citi, which reiterates a buy rating and A$2.55 target on the stock. "Positively, PRN continues to replenish and build on its robust secured work," analyst William Park says in a note. Citi estimates Perenti's work in hand could be as much as A$6 billion. The company also has plenty of room within existing capital expenditure guidance to chase new contract awards, Park adds. "We believe there is no shortage of opportunities that could potentially be crystallized in favor of PRN in FY26 including, but not limited to, Cowal, Geita, and Red Chris," he says. Perenti is down 1.0% at A$2.475, following a 4.2% gain Thursday. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1922 ET [Dow Jones]--Lithium miner Pilbara Minerals should deliver a relatively smooth operational performance in FY 2026, according to Jarden analysts Ben Lyons and Adam Bennett. The year ahead is "free of the major expansion tie-ins that impacted FY25, and with maintenance shutdowns scheduled on a quarterly basis," they say. The analysts reckon Pilbara Minerals will break even on a cash basis in FY 2026 and FY 2027 at a US$900/metric ton spodumene price. The stock remains Jarden's preferred Australian lithium exposure. The bank views it as a core holding in the resources sector, the analysts say. They reiterate an overweight rating and A$2.20 target. The stock last traded at A$1.97. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com)

1905 ET - Morgans's big takeaway from a visit to Aeris Resources' Tritton copper mine: the operation is worth more than it thought. Morgans now values Tritton at A$268 million, up from A$181 million before. That view is driven a shift in its assumptions from FY27 as processing of ore from the Constellation deposit starts. Morgans raises expectations for the copper head grade by 0.1% from FY27. "Given the copper grade at Constellation is 25% higher than the greater Tritton resources (ex Constellation) we think this is conservative," analyst Ross Bennett says. "In a scenario where head grade lifts to 1.9% copper we forecast 32,500 tons copper production in FY 2028/FY 2029." Morgans raises its price target on Aeris by 39%, to A$0.43/share. Aeris ended Thursday at A$0.325. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1440 ET - Wednesday's record close in gold appears to be the newest high watermark, at least now. Front-month gold futures finish down 0.2% to $3,636.90 a troy ounce, but that still the fourth-highest close in the front-month contract's history. The luster hasn't diminished from the outlook for gold's performance, says Trevor Yates of Global X ETFs in a note. "The worsening global debt outlook continues to support its relative appeal as a reserve asset for central banks," says Yates. SPDR Gold shares are down 0.2% in afternoon trading. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1331 ET - As farmers look towards harvesting the crops they planted this past spring and begin to plan for next spring's acres, rising prices for fertilizer are creating stress for the bottom line, says the American Farmer Bureau Federation in a note. "Farmers are facing a familiar challenge: building budgets and making planting decisions with unpredictable fertilizer markets," says Faith Parum, an economist with the AFBF. Affecting prices this year is the onset of U.S. tariffs on the exports of countries where a lot of fertilizer ingredients come from, including nitrogen and phosphate. "A small number of countries dominate nitrogen, phosphate and potash production, leaving supply chains exposed to geopolitical or logistical disruptions," says Parum. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1611 GMT - Amazon Autos hasn't hit top gear yet, especially in used vehicles, say Davidson analysts Tom White and Wyatt Swanson. Amazon Autos was launched in 2023 with a partnership with Hyundai for new vehicles, but the analysts say that Hyundai dealers are still seeing very limited sales through Amazon. Early last month, Amazon said dealers would be able to list their used vehicles on Amazon Autos, but " there remains relatively scant Used inventory available on the Amazon Autos platform," the analysts say. Almost all of the used inventory that is available is from Hertz, which recently said it was partnering with Amazon, and participating Hyundai dealers, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1526 GMT - Tariffs were expected to hit the auto industry hard, "yet six months into the onset of tariffs, we've been positively surprised by the extent to which the industry has held in better than anticipated," Barclays analysts Dan Levy and Josh Cho say in a research note, citing minimal disruption to production and sales, as well as ongoing consumer strength. Barclays upgrades its sector view on the U.S. autos and mobility to neutral from negative, as tariffs are now well understood by the market and likely to ease with additional trade deals. General Motors and Aptiv are especially well positioned, the analysts note, upgrading each to overweight from equal weight. GM should benefit from easing electric-vehicle regulations and resiliency in pricing, while parts supplier Aptiv can benefit from improved business dynamics alongside reasonable valuation, the analysts say. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1257 GMT - Mercedes-Benz could see some EBIT improvement by 2027, but shares have catalysts for improvement in 2026, Citi analysts write. EBIT margins have fallen by around 1,000 basis points on a combination of China market challenges, volume and pricing declines, U.S. tariffs, and the poor performance of previous EV investments. The company is now responding by cutting fixed and variable costs and a new S class model cycle should drive some on-year improvement in 2027, Citi adds. "We think Mercedes should commit to a fixed rather than a free cash flow-based annual return and restart the buyback to signal long-term confidence." Shares fall 1.5%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1233 GMT - BMW said this week that some of its assumptions regarding second-half earnings had changed, which Citi analysts say will focus investor minds. A delay in signing the EU-U.S. tariff agreement means manufacturers are paying 27.5% rather than 15% on imports for longer--potentially costing BMW around 200 million euros in third-quarter EBIT, the bank estimates. More worrying is that BMW's China assumptions have again proven too optimistic, resulting in a sharper China sales decline in 2025 and dealer compensation payments hitting margins, Citi says. Despite cost cuts and a better performance in other regions, "the worry remains that BMW is no longer competitive in China and the deterioration continues to weigh on earnings going forward." Shares fall 1.5%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1203 GMT - Transat A.T. faces a number of headwinds in the near-term, but TD Cowen's Tim James says in a note that he doesn't believe they should shift investor focus from attractive valuation when looking out over the next 12 months. The analyst says drags related to demand softness, increased competition and supply were themes in the F3Q report, but the airline's Elevation cost and revenue enhancement strategy, debt reduction, partnership with Porter airlines, along with improving demand "should allow Transat to generate structurally higher earnings despite short-term headwinds." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0820 GMT - China Eastern Airlines' outlook could remain challenging, says DBS Group Research's Paul Yong in a note. He expects the airline's profitability to remain under strain as passenger yield pressures endure, driven by fare wars, muted business travel demand and fiercer competition from China's growing high-speed rail network. He lowers his 2025 and 2026 earnings forecasts for the airline by 47% and 52%, respectively. DBS trims its target on China Eastern's A shares to CNY4.10 from CNY4.30, and on its H shares to HK$2.90 from HK$3.10, while maintaining its hold rating. China Eastern's Shanghai-listed shares closed 1.2% lower at CNY4.03, while its Hong Kong-listed stock ended flat at HK$2.97. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

2240 GMT - Risks to Mainfreight's earnings tilt to the downside, which may prolong the company's three-year cycle of profit downgrades, Forsyth Barr says. Mainfreight's pretax profit fell 24% in the first 17 weeks of FY 2026. That puts Mainfreight in catch-up mode, but analyst Andy Bowley says it becomes tougher to beat its performance a year ago as FY 2026 progresses. Forsyth Barr expects Mainfreight's annual pretax profit to total NZ$350 million. This implies the on-year decline narrows to 4% for the remaining 35 weeks. "We continue to believe this is achievable but requires better margin conversion, particularly in Transport and Warehousing, than that highlighted at the annual shareholder meeting, along with consistent new customer wins," Forsyth Barr says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1847 GMT - United Airlines Chief Executive Scott Kirby sees consolidation across the airline industry in the near term. At a Morgan Stanley conference, the executive said he expects that, within five years, there will be two mainline carriers with brand loyalty, a couple airlines serving mid-size and small cities, and a constant churn of low-cost carriers. Kirby sees United as one of those top two carriers because it's been investing in loyalty programs for roughly a decade. "If you haven't spent a decade or more building out a customer-friendly model, you can't fix that overnight," he said. "That's what I think is going to determine the winners and losers." (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1837 GMT - Premium flying demand is still holding strong for American Airlines while business travel remains soft, executives say at the Morgan Stanley Laguna Conference. Business traffic has gotten a little bit of a tailwind as the company wins back corporate share through its sales and distribution strategy, the executives say. But so far this month, it has held pretty firm at levels seen coming out of August, which is a slow month for business travel, they say. Premium meanwhile "has just been a star throughout," one executive says. That seems to be a secular trend that has continued despite difficult times in the economy, they say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1814 GMT - American Airlines had to contend with a "terrible July" but has seen demand get progressively better each month since, Chief Strategy Officer Steve Johnson says at the Morgan Stanley Laguna Conference. Bookings started to firm up around the July 4 weekend, resulting in a stronger August, and then September was better than August, Johnson says. While only 50% booked so far, October is shaping up to be better than September, he says. That's leaving the company very comfortable with its 3Q revenue guidance, which it should hit despite slightly lower capacity, Johnson says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1811 GMT - Southwest Airlines CEO Robert Jordan says a rise in demand for premium travel offerings appears to be more permanent than he might have previously thought. Coming out of the Covid-19 pandemic, travelers have shown an increasing appetite for long-haul international flights and lounge access, he says during the Morgan Stanley Laguna Conference. The carrier is looking to meet that demand, partnering with international airlines and looking to offer its own premium lounges, he says. If "you want a lounge, you want premium, you want extra legroom, you want to fly to London, whatever the list is, and we can't provide it, you are going to pick another carrier," he says. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1809 GMT - Southwest Airlines CEO Robert Jordan says the carrier aims to meet rising demand for long-haul international flights with recent partnerships, and hopes to serve such routes itself in the future. The carrier has launched recent partnerships with international airlines like Icelandair, China Airlines and EVA Air, and has many more in the pipeline ahead, he says during the Morgan Stanley Laguna Conference. Eventually, Southwest hopes to serve those destinations by itself, he says. "If we're going to continue to drive relevance, even as the largest domestic carrier, we've got to continue to meet the needs of our customer," he says. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: The Week in Oil: Market Pulled Between Supply Glut Worries, Geopolitical Risks
data.description:
By Giulia Petroni

Here's a look at what happened in oil markets in the week of Sept. 8-12 and what the focus will be in the days to come.

OVERVIEW: Oil prices have fluctuated between gains and losses this week amid concerns over a looming supply surplus and rising geopolitical risks. In evening trade in Europe, Brent crude and West Texas Intermediate hovered around $67 and $63 a barrel, respectively. Both benchmarks are on track for weekly gains of around 3% after the U.K. unveiled new sanctions targeting Russia's oil flows and a Ukrainian drone attack on a Russian port reportedly forced the suspension of loadings.

MACRO: The macroeconomic outlook is bullish for oil, with the Federal Reserve expected to cut interest rates by 25 basis points next week. Lower rates make borrowing cheaper, encouraging economic activity and typically leading to higher demand for crude.

The labor market weakness is fueling dovish bets, with jobless claims hitting the highest level since October 2021. Consumer prices continued to rise last month--above economists' expectations--but they did little to alter market expectations of imminent cuts.

GEOPOLITICAL RISKS: Heightened geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and Eastern Europe provided underlying support to prices this week, despite bearish fundamentals.

Israel attacked Hamas's leadership in Qatar, complicating regional dynamics. Meanwhile, Russian supplies have been under the spotlight amid intensifying Ukrainian strikes on key infrastructure and prospects of tougher Western sanctions.

"Fundamentals have so far been unaffected by moves to target Russia's oil exports due to reorienting supply chain, sanctions evasion and, more recently, India's willingness to continue importing Russian crude despite higher U.S. tariffs," said Kieran Tompkins from Capital Economics.

However, pressure from Western nations is intensifying.

The U.K. unveiled a fresh package of 100 sanctions targeting ships carrying Russian oil and companies supplying electronics, chemicals and explosives used to manufacture weapons.

Meanwhile, the EU is currently preparing a 19th package of sanctions against Russia and reportedly considering a faster phase-out of Russian fossil fuels. Also, two EU officials said President Trump asked the bloc to hit some goods from China and India--two major buyers of Russian oil--with tariffs of up to 100%.

SUPPLY AND DEMAND: Persistent concerns over a looming supply surplus are putting pressure on prices, with the International Energy agency now projecting higher supply growth this year and next.

The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries instead left its oil-demand forecasts unchanged after agreeing to raise production again next month, doubling down on its strategy shift in a push for market share.

"While OPEC+ has slowed the pace of production hikes compared with earlier months, its focus on reclaiming market share continues to pressure sentiment," analysts at MUFG said earlier this week.

Meanwhile, U.S. crude inventories also rose for a second straight week, underscoring softer demand in the world's largest consumer. "The latest EIA [Energy Information Administration] snapshot showed a sizeable crude build alongside very high refinery utilization-evidence that domestic supply remains ample even with peak summer runs now ebbing," said Felipe Barragan, strategist at Pepperstone. "Elevated stocks blunt the price impact of geopolitical scares and make rallies harder to sustain without a clean demand impulse."

NEXT WEEK: For the time being, oil prices are unlikely to move significantly, as the drag from increased OPEC+ production is countered by persistent geopolitical tensions, market watchers said.

Traders will continue to closely monitor geopolitical developments, with media reports saying the U.S. plans to urge its Group of Seven allies to impose tariffs on China and India over their purchases of Russian oil.

The September Fed meeting kicks off next week, with a policy decision expected on Wednesday. "Assuming the Fed does cut, markets will pay close attention to the updated statement and press conference with Powell to see whether this is the first of several cuts or if the guidance is more guarded around inflation risks," Saxo analysts said.

data.title: Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: Fed Set to Cut Rates; Policy Decisions Due in Japan, Canada, U.K.
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires staff

Below are the most important global events likely to affect FX and bond markets in the week starting Sept. 15.

A meeting of the U.S. Federal Reserve is widely expected to result in an interest-rate cut after a long pause, with investors likely to focus on the pace of rate reductions after that.

Interest-rate decisions are also due during the week in Japan, Canada and the U.K., among others.

Investors will parse through a raft of economic data releases from China for clues on how the world's second-largest economy is grappling with slowing global growth amid tariff-related uncertainties.

U.S.

The Federal Reserve announces a decision on Wednesday and is widely expected to resume cutting interest rates after recent data revealed weak U.S. jobs data.

A 25 basis-point rate reduction to 4.00-4.25% is widely anticipated, although there is an outside chance of a larger 50 basis-point cut.

Concerns about labor-market weakness are expected to offset worries about inflation. Policymakers might consider that price pressures remain contained for now although they will likely acknowledge risks.

"Despite inflation running above the Fed's 2% target, last week's weaker-than-expected nonfarm payroll figures, coupled with revised data showing 911,000 fewer jobs created in the 12 months to March, have strengthened the likelihood of monetary policy easing," said Richard Flax, chief investment officer at Moneyfarm, in a note.

Investors will also scrutinize accompanying comments for any signals on how fast rates will fall from here. U.S. money markets almost fully price in follow-up rate reductions in October and December, LSEG data show.

Analysts at Citi expect that signs of cooling inflation will keep policymakers focused on supporting a weak labor market. They anticipate five consecutive rate reductions to 3.00%-3.25%, but said it is possible rates could fall below 3.00%.

However, TD Securities strategists said Fed Chair Jerome Powell could signal that the Fed "isn't on a pre-determined path to cut interest rates and will keep monitoring incoming data."

President Trump has called for the Fed to cut interest rates over recent months, raising concerns among investors about the central bank's independence being eroded. Trump recently moved to fire Fed governor Lisa Cook and his administration is racing to sideline her before next week's policy meeting.

Other U.S. economic data due during the week include August retail sales and industrial production on Tuesday, followed by August housing starts on Wednesday and weekly jobless claims figures Thursday.

Canada

The Bank of Canada is expected to reduce interest rates by 25 basis points to 2.5% in a decision on Wednesday due to data showing a weak labor market, concerns about the economic impact of U.S. trade tariffs and evidence of relatively contained inflation.

Ahead of the decision, Canadian CPI inflation figures for August are due Tuesday.

These figures are unlikely to stop the BOC from cutting its policy rate, analysts at TD Securities said in a note.

"We see a high bar for this data to impact the bank's decision on rates," they said, adding that it would require a "sharp rebound" across various measures of core CPI to raise any doubts about a prospective rate cut.

Canadian retail sales data are due Friday, while August housing starts figures are released Tuesday.

Latin America

Brazil's central bank announces a decision on Wednesday and is expected to leave the Selic rate unchanged at 15.0%, pausing after recent rate increases.

"As things stand, we think it's most likely that Copom leaves the Selic rate at 15.00% until the turn of the year," said Capital Economics emerging market economist Kimberley Sperrfechter in a note.

Policymakers will be monitoring moves in the Brazilian currency amid concerns about the impact of tariffs, however, she said.

Eurozone

Germany's ZEW economic sentiment data for September are due on Tuesday.

Second-estimate Italian CPI inflation for August is due on Tuesday, while the equivalent data for the eurozone are scheduled for Wednesday.

"We expect [eurozone inflation] to remain unchanged from its flash estimates," HSBC analysts said in a note. "While the flash headline CPI print surprised slightly on the upside, we still believe that overall inflation will fall 2% next year, due to the stronger euro," they said.

Germany publishes producer price inflation data for August on Friday, while Spain releases industrial orders figures for July. Also on Friday, France's monthly business survey for September is due, a key insight into businesses' well-being in light of the recent change in government.

The Eurogroup meeting of eurozone finance ministers, as well as an informal Ecofin meeting of EU finance ministers, is due on Friday.

The German Finance Agency will publish its quarterly funding review on Thursday.

Germany will auction October 2030 Bobl on Tuesday, and August 2038 and August 2056 Bunds on Wednesday.

Other sovereign-bond issuers are Slovakia and Finland on Tuesday, Greece on Wednesday, and Spain and France on Thursday.

U.K.

The Bank of England announces an interest-rate decision on Thursday and is widely expected to leave rates on hold at 4.0% due to elevated inflation.

Focus will center on any signals about when interest rates could fall again. U.K. money markets currently only price in around a 36% chance of another interest-rate cut in 2025. Some analysts see a risk of another reduction in the coming months, however, due to the U.K.'s weak economy.

In that regard, U.K. jobs and wages data on Tuesday, followed by August inflation figures Wednesday, will be closely scrutinized ahead of the BOE's announcement.

Investec economist Philip Shaw said policymakers still have "collective doubts over whether the inflationary coast is clear to resume easing" and this could well prevent rates from rising for the rest of this year. Rate cuts should resume next year, however, he said.

The BOE is also expected to make a decision on quantitative tightening and to announce a reduction in the pace of sovereign-bond sales.

U.K. retail sales and public finances data for August are due Friday, alongside the GfK consumer confidence indicator for September.

The U.K. plans to sell government bonds maturing in January 2040 on Tuesday.

Scandinavia

Norway announces an interest-rate decision on Thursday, where most expect a 25 basis-point reduction in the key rate to 4.0%. A rate cut isn't a done deal, however, after higher-than-expected inflation data for August raise some doubts about this prospect.

"We expect the Norges Bank to cut its policy rate for the second time this year next week, but we see it as a close call," Citi analysts said in a note.

Sweden will auction inflation-linked bonds on Thursday.

South Africa

The South African Reserve Bank announces an interest rate decision on Thursday.

Citi analysts expect the main repo rate to be kept at 7.0%.

"The SARB is likely to retain a tolerance for inflation in its rhetoric, but this must not be mistaken for a dovish stance in our view; we forecast rates unchanged as the SARB aims for its new lower preferred target of 3.0% over the next 18-24 months," they said.

Japan

The Bank of Japan is expected to leave interest rates unchanged at 0.5% at its two-day meeting ending Friday as domestic political uncertainty rises. Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba's decision to step down has fueled speculation that a new premier could favor easier monetary policy.

Economists still expect the central bank to resume tightening soon as inflation proves sticky and trade headwinds ease. Data for the week are likely to underscore persistent price pressures: Consumer prices excluding fresh food are expected to have risen 2.7% in August from a year earlier, down from July's 3.1% gain, according to a poll by data provider Quick.

Japan will also release August trade balance figures. The Ministry of Finance is set to auction 800 billion yen of 20-year Japanese government bonds on Wednesday, alongside sales of 1-year treasury discount bills and three-month bills on Thursday. Markets will be closed Monday for a national holiday.

Australia/New Zealand

In Australia, bond markets will focus on August employment data due Thursday, amid concerns that the labor market may be at a turning point. With government hiring cooling, the private sector is under pressure to sustain job growth. Economists say there is a growing risk that unemployment will move higher over the coming months. Uncertainty in the global economy could be a reason for why the private sector becomes hesitant to add employees, they say.

New Zealand will release second-quarter GDP data on Thursday, with economists expecting a 0.3% contraction, leaving growth flat for the year. Despite 250 basis points of rate cuts since mid-2024, recession risks remain. Economists expect the Reserve Bank of New Zealand to continue easing, with the next policy meeting set for early October.

China

A slate of August data is in focus for China, as markets look for insight into how the economy is faring midway through the third quarter. The country will announce figures for industrial output and retail sales on Monday, along with its latest figures for fixed assets investment and housing prices.

Economists in a Wall Street Journal poll project industrial production edged up to 5.8% on year last month. Retail sales are expected to have improved to 4.4% on year, signalling improvement in consumer demand. But fixed-asset investment is projected to have slowed to 1.3% in the year to date, raising concerns that a dip below 1% could prompt further policy support, Citi economists note.

Housing price data and property investment figures will be closely watched for signs of stabilization. ING economists expect housing prices to confirm a continued downturn, noting that August will test whether July's weakness was weather-related or the start of a deeper slump.

Indonesia

Bank Indonesia will announce its policy decision Wednesday and is widely expected to hold rates steady after two consecutive cuts. The central bank has stepped in to stabilize the rupiah and may pause for further easing until volatility subsides, Maybank analysts Brian Lee Shun Rong and Chua Hak Bin wrote. They added that the central bank is also deploying liquidity tools such as government-bond purchases and short-term securities for further support.

Maybank still expects 50 basis points of additional cuts by year-end, bringing the benchmark rate to 5% in 2025 and 4.5% by end-2026, in line with expectations of a weaker greenback amid anticipated Fed rate cuts.

Taiwan

Taiwan's central bank will announce its quarterly policy rate decision Thursday, with economists unanimously expecting rates to be held steady. Inflation pressures have been moderate, while growth remains strong thanks to robust external demand.

ANZ recently lifted its full-year GDP growth forecast for Taiwan to 6.2% for 2025. Markets will look for signals on the timing of future cuts. Barclays economists see the earliest window for easing in December, followed by two more cuts in the first half of 2026.

India

India's August trade data is the main focus this week as new U.S. tariffs cast a shadow over the economic outlook. While the overall hit may be limited for the largely domestic-driven economy, ING economists caution that India can't afford to downplay its reliance on the U.S., which accounts for 21% of its total exports. Labor-intensive sectors such as textiles, leather and jewellery are especially vulnerable given thin margins and price-sensitivity, ING said.

ING also doubts Asia can absorb the shortfall, noting that India's trade with the region has been shrinking. Markets will be watching for signs in August's data that tariffs are already affecting flows.

Also on Monday, wholesale price index figures for August will offer another gauge of inflation, which has been on a steady cooling trend.

Singapore

Singapore will publish its August non-oil domestic exports on Wednesday. July's exports fell 4.6% from a year earlier, and markets are watching for signs of a rebound. Barclays expects growth to have picked up in August due to a favorable base effect, though sequential momentum likely remained weak.

Malaysia

Malaysia will release August's trade data. Barclays expects export growth to have slowed after July's surprise strength, narrowing the trade surplus.

Looking ahead, exports may remain on choppy waters as risks shift from reciprocal tariffs to product-specific measures. U.S. President Trump's proposal for "fairly substantial" tariffs on semiconductor imports poses a threat for Malaysia, a key global supplier, TA Securities analyst Chan Mun Chun noted.

Any references to days are in local times.

Write to Jessica Fleetham at jessica.fleetham@wsj.com and Jihye Lee at jihye.lee@wsj.com

In evening trade, the benchmark Dutch TTF contract rises 1.2% to 32.71 euros a megawatt hour. "The recent violation of Polish airspace by Russian drones has raised the prospect of the U.S. and Europe further restricting Moscow's energy exports," ANZ analysts say. According to media reports, the U.S. will urge its allies in the Group of Seven to impose tariffs on China and India for their purchases of Russian oil. On the other hand, EU gas stockpiles are steadily building, making investors confident that members of the bloc should reach their filling targets before the winter season begins. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1033 ET - Oil futures are recovering from the previous day's losses as the U.K. announces new sanctions on Russia and the U.S. urges Europe to put tariff pressure on buyers of Russian oil, namely India and China. "The Russia/Ukraine war remains a positive to crude prices with Ukraine hitting a major oil export facility in Russia late yesterday," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. "Russia exports nearly 7 million barrels a day, therefore any disruptions whether from tariffs or war conflicts could be a bullish catalyst for crude prices near-term." WTI is up 1.5% at $63.28 a barrel and Brent is up 1.6% at $67.43. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0957 ET - Today's WASDE report from the USDA will include fresh projections for U.S. beef and pork, which will likely create movements in futures come next week. For cattle, futures are slightly higher, but prices remain roughly 8 cents a pound off from the record of $2.40 a pound set late last month. "On one hand, the futures recovery has seemingly improved overall sentiment, but on the other hand we're still watching both spot cash cattle and cutout markets trade lower this week," says StoneX in a note. Live cattle rises 0.3%, lean hogs slip 0.2%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0954 ET - U.S. natural gas futures remain in a choppy trade around the $3 level with rally attempts held back by surplus inventories heading into the low-demand shoulder season. Yesterday's EIA report showed storage up 71 Bcf at 3,343 Bcf, or 188 Bcf above the five-year average. "A late-season hurricane or mild October pose risks for a storage overbuild and rapidly deteriorating gas prices," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. But "eventually, even normal October weather may allow a bullish weather-normalized supply/demand balance to rapidly winnow storage surpluses to the five-year norm." Nymex natural gas is up 2.1% at $2.995/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0925 ET - CBOT grain futures have a mixed showing in premarket trade ahead of this afternoon's WASDE report. Analysts surveyed by WSJ this week are forecasting small decreases to the outlook for corn and soybean production and yields, but in the case of corn, output is expected to remain well above record highs. Strong figures reported by Brazilian crop agency Conab for that country's harvest are also keeping pre-WASDE trading tentative. Most-active CBOT corn is up 0.4%, soybeans fall 0.1%, and wheat drops 0.5%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0604 ET - Palm oil prices closed lower on weaker soybean oil prices, says David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. He notes there are concerns over rising stock level in the country, pegging support for CPO futures at 4,400 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,580 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for November delivery closed 9 ringgit lower at 4,445 ringgit a ton. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0443 ET - Mining stocks have helped propel the FTSE 100 index to a record high in morning trade in London. Anglo American shares are up nearly 13% over the past 5 days on the news that it has agreed to merge with Teck Resources to create a $53 billion copper giant. Glencore is up 7%. Much of the share-price growth has been concentrated on gold and silver stocks as precious metals continue to benefit from safe-have demand. Fresnillo trades up 7%, Hochschild Mining is up 6%, with Endeavour Mining 4% higher. The FTSE 100 trades up 0.3% at 9,326 points. Over the year-to-date, it is up 14%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0426 ET - Copper prices are set for strong weekly gains as operations at a large mine in Indonesia remain suspended, raising supply concerns. In early trade, three-month copper on the London Metal Exchange rises 0.5% to $10,110.50 a metric ton and is up 2.5% on the week. Phoenix-based miner Freeport-McMoRan said mining operations at the Grasberg site in Central Papua remain temporarily suspended after a mud flow incident left seven workers trapped underground. "A prolonged disruption at the second largest copper mine in the world could tighten the market," ANZ analysts say. Meanwhile, expectations of easing monetary policy in the U.S. are lifting sentiment across the industrial metals sector and brightening demand prospects. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0409 ET - Oil prices fall in early trade, extending the previous session's losses as concerns over looming oversupply and softening U.S demand overshadow geopolitical risks in the Middle East and Eastern Europe. Brent crude slips 0.6% to $65.95 a barrel, while WTI is down 0.7% to $61.95 a barrel. The IEA now expects global supply to rise faster than previously forecast, far exceeding demand growth as OPEC+ continues to raise output. U.S. crude inventories also rose for a second straight week, underscoring softer demand in the world's largest consumer. The retreat follows days of gains fueled by prospects of stricter Western sanctions on Russian oil exports after President Trump asked the EU to hit some Chinese and Indian goods with tariffs in a new attempt to ratchet up pressure on the Kremlin. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0342 ET - European oil stocks slip in opening trade as worries about oversupply continue to mount. Brent crude trades down 0.7% at $65.93 a barrel and prices remain under pressure. A surprise inventory build of 3.9 million barrels in the U.S. last week combined with growing concerns around oversupply as OPEC+ members plan to boost production is contributing to traders' worries, Derren Nathan, head of equity research at Hargreaves Lansdown, writes. In London, BP shares fall 0.7% and Shell drops 0.15%. France's TotalEnergies is down 0.65% while shares in Spain's Repsol and Italy's Eni are down around 0.4%. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0342 ET - Gold prices are poised for a fourth straight weekly gain on expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates Wednesday. Futures rise 0.4% to $3,689.80 a troy ounce in early trade and are up 1% on the week. Markets are pricing in a 25-basis-point cut next week and potentially further easing by year-end. Non-yielding bullion tends to benefit in low-interest environments. "Softer U.S. labor data and an in-line August inflation print have given policymakers room to ease, while a weaker dollar and falling Treasury yields further support gold," says Soojin Kim, analyst at MUFG. "Gold has surged 39% this year, outpacing equities, with demand reinforced by central bank purchases, geopolitical uncertainty, and inflows into ETFs." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)
